name: Egyptian Expanded 2
description: Comprehensive expansion of Egyptian mythology with additional gods, heroes, and mythological beings.

beings.name: Osiris
beings.summary: God of death and resurrection who judges souls and rules the underworld. Osiris was murdered by his brother Set but resurrected through Isis' magic to become judge of the dead. He represents the Egyptian understanding of death as transition rather than end, and the possibility of renewal through divine intervention.

beings.name: Isis
beings.summary: Goddess of magic and motherhood who resurrected Osiris and protected Horus. Isis used her magical knowledge to resurrect her murdered husband Osiris and protect their son Horus from Set's attacks. She represents the Egyptian ideal of feminine wisdom and the power of magic to overcome death and evil.

beings.name: Set
beings.summary: God of chaos and desert who murdered his brother Osiris and opposed Horus. Set represents the destructive forces of the desert and chaos that constantly threaten the ordered world of the gods. He dismembers Osiris and attempts to kill Horus, embodying the eternal struggle between order and chaos in Egyptian cosmology.

beings.name: Horus
beings.summary: Sky god and avenger of his father Osiris who defeated Set and became king. Horus was raised by Isis to avenge his father Osiris' murder by Set. He defeated Set in combat and became the rightful king of the gods, representing the triumph of order over chaos and divine justice.

beings.name: Thoth
beings.summary: God of wisdom and writing who maintains cosmic order and records divine judgments. Thoth invented writing and mathematics, serving as the scribe of the gods and mediator in their disputes. He represents the Egyptian value of knowledge and the power of writing to preserve eternal truths.

beings.name: Anubis
beings.summary: Jackal-headed god who guides souls through the underworld and mummifies the dead. Anubis weighs the hearts of the dead against the feather of truth, determining their fate in the afterlife. He represents the Egyptian understanding of death as a journey requiring guidance and judgment.

beings.name: Sekhmet
beings.summary: Lioness goddess of war and plague who protects Ra and destroys his enemies. Sekhmet embodies the destructive power of the sun and the fierce protection of divine order. She spreads plague when Ra needs to punish humanity, representing the dual nature of divine power as both protective and destructive.

beings.name: Bastet
beings.summary: Cat goddess of protection and music who guards against evil and brings joy. Bastet protects households from evil spirits and disease while bringing music and dance to celebrations. She represents the Egyptian appreciation for cats as protective forces and the joy of domestic life.

beings.name: Ptah
beings.summary: Creator god who spoke the world into existence and invented crafts. Ptah created the world through his thoughts and words, then invented all crafts and arts. He represents the Egyptian understanding of creation through divine speech and the sacred nature of craftsmanship.

beings.name: Neith
beings.summary: Ancient goddess of weaving and war who created the world and protects the dead. Neith is one of the oldest Egyptian deities, credited with creating the world through her weaving. She also protects the dead and was worshipped by warriors, representing the connection between creation and destruction.

beings.name: Ra
beings.summary: Sun god who travels through the underworld each night and creates all life. Ra sails across the sky in his solar boat, then travels through the underworld at night, facing constant threats from chaos forces. He represents the eternal cycle of day and night and the creative power of the sun.

beings.name: Amun
beings.summary: Hidden god of creation and wind who became supreme deity through Theban power. Amun represents the invisible creative force and the hidden aspects of divinity. He merged with Ra to become Amun-Ra, combining hidden and visible aspects of divine power. Represents the Egyptian understanding of divine mystery.

beings.name: Hathor
beings.summary: Sky goddess of love and music who nurses kings and protects the dead. Hathor appears as a woman with cow horns, nursing both divine kings and human children. She represents the nurturing aspects of the divine and the connection between sky and earth through maternal care.

beings.name: Sobek
beings.summary: Crocodile god of the Nile who controls military power and fertility. Sobek represents the dangerous and fertile aspects of the Nile River, which could both nurture crops and destroy through floods. He was worshipped by soldiers and represented the military's connection to the Nile's power.

beings.name: Khnum
beings.summary: Ram-headed god who creates humans on his potter's wheel and guards the Nile source. Khnum molds human bodies and souls on his potter's wheel at the Nile's source. He represents the Egyptian understanding of human creation as divine craftsmanship and the sacred origin of life.

beings.name: Geb
beings.summary: Earth god who lies beneath the sky goddess Nut and supports all terrestrial life. Geb is the earth itself, separated from his sister-wife Nut to allow space for creation. He represents the foundation of all existence and the Egyptian understanding of earth as living divine being.

beings.name: Nut
beings.summary: Sky goddess who arches over the earth and gives birth to the sun each dawn. Nut was separated from her brother-husband Geb to create space for the world. She swallows the sun each evening and gives birth to it each dawn, representing the eternal cycle of day and night.

stories.name: Murder and Resurrection of Osiris
stories.summary: Set murders and dismembers his brother Osiris, who is resurrected by Isis' magic.
stories.description: Set murders his brother Osiris out of jealousy and dismembers his body, scattering the pieces across Egypt. Isis gathers the pieces and uses her magic to resurrect Osiris, who then becomes ruler of the underworld.

stories.name: Horus and Set's Cosmic Battle
stories.summary: Son avenges father by defeating evil uncle in cosmic combat for kingship.
stories.description: Horus, raised by Isis to avenge his father Osiris, battles his uncle Set for control of the cosmos. After eighty years of conflict, Horus defeats Set and becomes the rightful king of the gods, restoring order to the universe.

stories.name: Ra's Nightly Underworld Journey
stories.summary: Sun god travels through the underworld each night, facing constant threats from chaos.
stories.description: Each night, Ra travels through the twelve hours of the underworld in his solar boat, facing constant attacks from chaos forces led by the serpent Apep. He triumphs through divine magic and the help of other gods, ensuring the sun's return each dawn.

stories.name: Isis Uses Magic to Rescue Osiris
stories.summary: Goddess uses magical knowledge and maternal determination to overcome death.
stories.description: After Set murders and dismembers Osiris, Isis searches Egypt for all the pieces of her husband's body. She uses her magical knowledge to reassemble and resurrect him, demonstrating the power of feminine wisdom and magic to overcome death.

stories.name: Thoth Invents Writing and Mathematics
stories.summary: Wisdom god creates writing to preserve eternal truths and divine knowledge.
stories.description: Thoth invents writing and mathematics to preserve divine knowledge and human wisdom for eternity. His invention allows the recording of history, laws, and religious texts, ensuring that wisdom survives beyond human memory.

stories.name: Khnum Creates Humans on Potter's Wheel
stories.summary: Creator god molds human bodies and souls on his divine potter's wheel.
stories.description: Khnum creates each human on his potter's wheel at the Nile's source, molding both body and soul from clay. He represents the Egyptian understanding of human creation as divine craftsmanship and the sacred origin of individual lives.

stories.name: Sekhmet Spreads Plague for Ra
stories.summary: Lioness goddess unleashes destructive power to punish humanity for Ra.
stories.description: When Ra needs to punish rebellious humanity, he sends Sekhmet to spread plague and destruction. She nearly destroys all humans until Ra tricks her with beer mixed with red dye, stopping her rampage. Represents divine power as both protective and destructive.

places.name: Abydos
places.summary: Sacred city where Osiris was buried and resurrected, center of his cult.
places.description: Ancient city where Osiris was buried and where Isis resurrected him. Abydos became the major center of Osiris worship and the site where kings sought association with the resurrected god.

places.name: Heliopolis
places.summary: City of the sun where Ra was worshipped and creation myths were recorded.
places.description: Ancient city dedicated to Ra worship and the center of solar theology. Creation myths and theological texts were recorded here, making it a major center of Egyptian religious thought and solar worship.

places.name: Memphis
places.summary: Capital city where Ptah created the world and maintained cosmic balance.
places.description: Ancient capital where Ptah spoke the world into existence and maintained cosmic order. Memphis was the political and religious center where creation theology was developed and the balance between gods was maintained.

places.name: Thebes
places.summary: City where Amun rose to supremacy and became hidden god of creation.
places.description: City where Amun became the supreme deity through Theban political power. Thebes was the center of Amun worship and the development of theology about the hidden aspects of divinity and cosmic mystery.

places.name: Egyptian Underworld
places.summary: Realm of the dead where Osiris judges souls and Anubis guides them.
places.description: Underworld realm where Osiris judges the dead with Anubis weighing their hearts against truth. The righteous proceed to blessed afterlife while the wicked are destroyed, representing Egyptian concepts of moral justice after death.

places.name: Source of the Nile
places.summary: Sacred place where Khnum creates humans on his potter's wheel.
places.description: Sacred location at the Nile's source where Khnum creates humans on his potter's wheel. It represents the divine origin of life and the sacred connection between the Nile's life-giving waters and human creation.

places.name: Duat
places.summary: Underworld region through which Ra travels nightly and souls are judged.
places.description: Underworld region that Ra travels through each night in his solar boat, facing constant threats from chaos forces. It's also where souls go for judgment, representing the dangerous journey between death and afterlife.

places.name: Field of Reeds
places.summary: Place where Isis found Osiris' body and where Moses was found as infant.
places.description: Marshy area where Isis discovered the coffin containing Osiris' body and where the infant Moses was later found among reeds. It represents the connection between divine rescue and human salvation stories.

motifs.name: Death and Resurrection Cycle
motifs.summary: Death is not final but part of eternal cycle of renewal and judgment.
motifs.description: Pattern where death leads to resurrection and judgment rather than final end. Osiris is murdered but resurrected to become underworld judge, representing the Egyptian understanding of death as transition to new existence rather than annihilation.

motifs.name: Order Versus Chaos Cosmic Struggle
motifs.summary: Cosmic order constantly defends against chaos forces that seek to destroy it.
motifs.description: Pattern where the ordered world of the gods constantly faces threats from chaos forces. Horus battles Set for cosmic control, Ra fights chaos nightly, representing the eternal struggle between creation and destruction.

motifs.name: Divine Craftsmanship and Creation
motifs.summary: Creation and divine power are expressed through skilled craftsmanship and art.
motifs.description: Pattern where divine creation and power are expressed through craftsmanship. Ptah creates the world through speech, Khnum molds humans on his potter's wheel, representing the sacred nature of human artistic and technical skills.

motifs.name: Solar Daily Journey and Nightly Underworld Travel
motifs.summary: Sun god travels across sky by day and through underworld each night.
motifs.description: Pattern where the sun god makes a daily journey across the sky and nightly journey through the underworld. Ra's eternal cycle represents the Egyptian understanding of cosmic order and the triumph of light over darkness.

motifs.name: Magic and Divine Feminine Wisdom
motifs.summary: Feminine divine power expresses through magic, wisdom, and protective love.
motifs.description: Pattern where feminine divine power works through magic, wisdom, and protective love. Isis uses magic to resurrect Osiris and protect Horus, representing the Egyptian valuing of feminine wisdom as protective and restorative force.

motifs.name: Moral Judgment After Death
motifs.summary: Souls face moral judgment after death based on their earthly actions.
motifs.description: Pattern where souls face moral judgment after death, with hearts weighed against truth. Anubis weighs souls while Osiris judges them, representing the Egyptian belief that earthly morality determines afterlife fate.

groups.name: Ennead
groups.summary: Nine gods of Heliopolis who form the primary divine council and creation family.

groups.name: Ogdoad
groups.summary: Eight primordial gods who emerged from chaos and established cosmic order.

groups.name: Funerary Priests
groups.summary: Religious specialists who prepare the dead for afterlife journey through mummification.

groups.name: Cult Followers
groups.summary: Devoted worshippers who maintain divine cults and serve the gods through ritual.

groups.name: Royal Artisans
groups.summary: Skilled craftsmen who create divine objects and maintain royal workshops.

objects.name: Ankh
objects.summary: Symbol of eternal life representing the breath of existence and divine immortality.

objects.name: Eye of Ra
objects.summary: Powerful divine eye that represents Ra's destructive vision and protective power.

objects.name: Crook and Flail
objects.summary: Royal symbols of shepherd leadership and divine authority over life and death.

objects.name: Book of the Dead
objects.summary: Magical funerary text guiding souls through afterlife judgment and providing protection.

sources.title: Pyramid Texts
sources.description: Religious texts inscribed in pyramid walls describing afterlife journey and divine protection.

sources.title: Coffin Texts
sources.description: Funerary texts painted on coffins providing afterlife guidance and magical protection.

sources.title: Hermopolitan Cosmogony
sources.description: Theological texts describing creation of the world and divine family relationships.

sources.title: Memphite Theology
sources.description: Theological texts focusing on Ptah as creator god and maintaining cosmic order through divine thought.

sources.title: Book of the Dead
sources.description: Comprehensive funerary text providing afterlife guidance, magical spells, and divine protection for the dead.
